Welcome to Your ESOP
Employee Guide — What You Need to Know
Company
{{companyName}}
Effective Date
{{closingDate}}
Prepared For
All {{companyName}} Employees
Reference
{{ref}}
What just happened? {{companyName}} is now an employee-owned company. Your employer has created an Employee Stock Ownership Plan (ESOP) that gives you a beneficial ownership stake in the company — at no cost to you. This booklet explains what that means.
§ 01What Is an ESOP?

An ESOP is a qualified retirement plan that holds shares of your company’s stock on behalf of employees. Think of it like a 401(k), but instead of holding mutual funds, it holds shares in {{companyName}}.

The Basics
1.You don’t buy anything. Unlike stock options or employee stock purchase plans, you never pay for your ESOP shares. The company funds the plan entirely.
2.Your account grows as the company grows. As {{companyName}} becomes more profitable and pays down debt, the value of your ESOP shares is expected to increase over time.
3.It’s a retirement benefit. Your ESOP account is a retirement savings vehicle, similar to a 401(k). You can’t access the money while you’re still working (with limited exceptions).
4.Nothing changes about your job. Your role, manager, pay, and benefits remain the same. ESOP ownership does not affect day-to-day operations.
§ 02How Your Account Works

Each year, the company may contribute shares or cash to the ESOP on your behalf. Those contributions are allocated to your individual account based on your compensation relative to total covered payroll.

A.Annual allocations. Each year the company makes a contribution, shares are added to your account. The amount depends on your salary and the company’s contribution level.
B.Valuation. An independent appraiser determines the fair market value of the company stock each year. This determines what your shares are worth.
C.Vesting. You become fully vested (i.e., you own the shares outright) after a period of service, typically 3–6 years. If you leave before you’re fully vested, you may forfeit some shares.
D.Statements. You will receive an annual statement showing how many shares are in your account and their estimated value. Your first statement will arrive within {{statementTimeline}} after the plan year closes.
§ 03When Do You Get Paid?

ESOP distributions generally happen when you leave the company (retirement, termination, disability, or death). The company may also offer in-service distributions after age 55, depending on the plan terms.

A.Distribution timing. After separation from service, distributions typically begin within one plan year and may be paid over 5 years (or longer for balances above a certain threshold).
B.Payment form. Distributions are made in cash or stock, as determined by the plan document. Most ESOPs distribute in cash.
C.Rolling over. You can roll your ESOP distribution into an IRA or another qualified plan, deferring taxes until you withdraw the funds.
D.Diversification rights. After certain milestones (typically age 55 with 10+ years of participation), you may have the right to diversify a portion of your account into investments other than company stock.
§ 04What Changes (and What Doesn’t)
What Stays the Same
✓Your job, title, and reporting structure
✓Your salary, benefits, and 401(k)
✓Your manager and day-to-day responsibilities
✓The company name, customers, and operations
What’s new: You now have a retirement account funded by the company that grows as the company grows. You also have a voice — the ESOP gives you certain rights to information about the plan and the company’s financial health.
§ 05Who Manages the ESOP?

The ESOP is managed by several parties, each with a specific role:

A.ESOP Trustee. An independent fiduciary who holds the shares on your behalf and ensures the plan operates in the best interest of participants. The trustee hired {{trusteeName}}.
B.Plan Administrator. Manages day-to-day plan operations — processing distributions, maintaining records, and answering your questions. Contact: {{planAdminName}}.
C.Company Management. Continues to run the business. The ESOP does not change who makes operational decisions.
D.ERISA Counsel. Legal advisors who ensure the plan complies with federal law.
§ 06Frequently Asked Questions
Q.Do I have to contribute my own money? No. The ESOP is 100% funded by the company. There is no payroll deduction.
Q.Does this affect my 401(k)? No. Your 401(k) continues unchanged. The ESOP is a separate retirement plan.
Q.What if the stock price goes down? ESOP accounts are subject to investment risk. The value of your account depends on the company’s performance. However, diversified ESOPs historically outperform conventional retirement plans over the long term.
Q.What happens if I leave the company? Your vested balance will be distributed to you according to the plan’s distribution schedule. You can roll it into an IRA.
Q.Do I get to vote on company decisions? For most matters, the trustee votes the shares on your behalf. You may have direct voting rights for certain major decisions (like mergers or dissolution).
Q.Is my job safe? The ESOP does not change employment relationships. The company continues to operate as before, with the same management team.
§ 07Who to Contact
{{planAdminName}} (Plan Administrator)
